name: Production Incident Response
description: Guides SRE/DevOps teams through structured incident response — from initial triage through root cause analysis and post-mortem

defaultWeights.label: Severity Bias
defaultWeights.description: How aggressively to escalate — high treats every incident as critical, low triages carefully

defaultWeights.label: Cost of Downtime
defaultWeights.description: Business impact of outage duration — high justifies expensive fixes, low manages expectations

defaultWeights.label: Root Cause Depth
defaultWeights.description: How deep to investigate root cause — high finds systemic issues, low fixes symptoms

defaultWeights.label: Blast Radius
defaultWeights.description: Concern for wider system impact — high checks all dependencies, low focuses on affected service

defaultWeights.label: Recovery Speed
defaultWeights.description: Time-to-recovery pressure — high rolls back fast, low diagnoses before acting

defaultWeights.label: Communication Level
defaultWeights.description: Stakeholder communication frequency — high updates constantly, low updates at milestones

defaultWeights.label: Change Freeze
defaultWeights.description: Post-incident caution — high enforces long freeze, low resumes deploys quickly

defaultWeights.label: Automation Trust
defaultWeights.description: Trust in automated remediation — high auto-heals, low requires human approval

nodes.label: Alert Source & Signal
nodes.description: Where did the alert originate and what signal triggered it
choices: PagerDuty / OpsGenie alert — automated monitoring threshold breached
Customer report — support tickets or social media reports of issues
Internal discovery — engineer noticed during routine work
Synthetic monitor — uptime check or canary failure
Dependency notification — upstream provider status page or email
Security alert — IDS/SIEM triggered, suspicious activity detected

nodes.label: Initial Severity Estimate
nodes.description: First responder's gut-level severity assessment
choices: SEV-1 — Complete outage, all users affected, revenue impact
SEV-2 — Major degradation, significant user impact, SLA at risk
SEV-3 — Partial degradation, subset of users affected
SEV-4 — Minor issue, workaround available, low user impact
Unknown — Not enough information to classify yet

nodes.label: Affected Service
nodes.description: Which service or system is experiencing the issue
nodes.prompt: Which service or system is affected? (e.g., 'payments-api', 'user-auth', 'CDN edge', 'database-primary')

nodes.label: Environment & Region
nodes.description: Which environment and geographic region is impacted
choices: Production — all regions
Production — single region (us-east, eu-west, ap-southeast, etc.)
Staging / Pre-production
Production — specific availability zone
Edge / CDN layer
Shared infrastructure (DNS, load balancer, service mesh)

nodes.label: Observable Symptoms
nodes.description: What symptoms are users or systems experiencing
nodes.prompt: Describe the observable symptoms (e.g., '500 errors on /api/checkout, p99 latency >5s, connection timeouts to DB')

nodes.label: Recent Changes
nodes.description: Any deployments, config changes, or infrastructure modifications in the last 24 hours
nodes.prompt: What changed in the last 24 hours?
choices: Code deployment — application release in last 24h
Config change — feature flag, env var, or config update
Infrastructure change — scaling, migration, cert rotation, DNS update
Dependency update — library, package, or upstream API version change
No known changes — nothing deployed or modified recently
Multiple changes — several of the above happened recently

nodes.label: System Architecture
nodes.description: What kind of architecture does the affected system use
nodes.prompt: What's the architecture of the affected system?
choices: Monolith — single deployable unit
Microservices — distributed services communicating via API/gRPC
Serverless — Lambda/Functions/Cloud Run
Event-driven — message queues, Kafka, event bus
Hybrid — mix of monolith and microservices
Static / CDN — static site or edge-cached content

nodes.label: Team Availability
nodes.description: Who is available to respond right now
choices: On-call engineer only — solo responder, business hours
On-call engineer only — solo responder, off-hours
Small team — 2-3 engineers available
Full incident team — IC, communications lead, and engineers
Cross-team — multiple team leads available for coordination

nodes.label: Monitoring & Observability
nodes.description: Assumes centralized logging, metrics dashboards, and distributed tracing are available
nodes.assumption: Centralized logging (ELK/Datadog/Splunk), metrics dashboards (Grafana/Datadog), and distributed tracing (Jaeger/Zipkin) are available and currently functioning

nodes.label: Runbooks Exist
nodes.description: Assumes documented runbooks or playbooks exist for common failure modes
nodes.assumption: The team has documented runbooks or playbooks for common failure modes of this service

nodes.label: Canary / Rollback Available
nodes.description: Assumes canary deployments or fast rollback mechanisms are in place
nodes.assumption: The deployment pipeline supports canary releases or one-click rollback to the previous known-good version

nodes.label: Severity Classification
nodes.description: Classifies the incident severity based on inputs and weight profile
nodes.condition: What is the confirmed severity level?

nodes.label: Open War Room
nodes.description: For SEV-1/SEV-2: establish incident command, open bridge call, assign roles
nodes.template: Incident: {{affectedService}} in {{environment}}. Severity: {{initialSeverity}}. Alert: {{alertSource}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Recent changes: {{recentChanges}}. Team: {{teamStatus}}. Monitoring: {{monitoringExists}}. Runbooks: {{runbooksExist}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are a senior SRE incident commander. A high-severity incident has been declared. Generate:
1. **Incident summary** — one-paragraph situation report
2. **Role assignments** — Incident Commander, Communications Lead, Technical Lead, Scribe
3. **Bridge call setup** — Zoom/Slack channel naming convention, stakeholder notification template
4. **First 5 actions** — ordered investigation steps based on the symptoms and architecture
5. **Communication template** — first external status page update (acknowledge, impact, ETA)

Be specific to the architecture type and symptoms described. Reference the weight profile.

nodes.label: Quick Triage
nodes.description: For SEV-3/SEV-4: lightweight investigation without full war room
nodes.template: Incident: {{affectedService}} in {{environment}}. Severity: {{initialSeverity}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Recent changes: {{recentChanges}}. Monitoring available: {{monitoringExists}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an experienced SRE doing quick triage on a lower-severity incident. Generate:
1. **Impact assessment** — who is affected and how
2. **Top 3 likely causes** — based on symptoms, architecture, and recent changes
3. **Quick diagnosis commands** — specific kubectl, AWS CLI, or log queries to run
4. **Workaround options** — temporary mitigations while investigating
5. **Escalation criteria** — what would bump this to SEV-2 or SEV-1

Be specific to the technology stack described.

nodes.label: Rollback Decision
nodes.description: Should we rollback immediately or investigate first?
nodes.condition: Were there recent changes that could be rolled back?

nodes.label: Execute Rollback
nodes.description: Roll back the most recent change and verify recovery
nodes.template: Service: {{affectedService}}. Change type: {{recentChanges}}. Canary/rollback available: {{canaryAvailable}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Environment: {{environment}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an SRE executing an emergency rollback. Generate:
1. **Rollback plan** — exact steps to revert the {{recentChanges}} for a {{architecture}} system
2. **Verification checklist** — how to confirm the rollback succeeded (health checks, metrics, smoke tests)
3. **Data safety** — any database migrations or state changes that complicate rollback
4. **Rollback commands** — specific CLI commands or pipeline triggers
5. **Monitoring watch points** — key metrics to watch for the next 30 minutes post-rollback

Consider whether canary rollback or full rollback is appropriate.

nodes.label: Failure Domain
nodes.description: Classify whether the issue is infrastructure, application, or external dependency
nodes.condition: What domain is the failure in?

nodes.label: Infrastructure Investigation
nodes.description: Investigate infrastructure-level issues: networking, compute, storage, DNS
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an infrastructure SRE investigating a potential infrastructure failure. Generate:
1. **Network checks** — DNS resolution, connectivity tests, security group/firewall verification, MTU issues
2. **Compute checks** — CPU/memory/disk pressure, OOM kills, instance health, auto-scaling state
3. **Storage checks** — disk I/O, IOPS limits, storage quota, replication lag
4. **Load balancer checks** — health check status, target group draining, certificate expiry
5. **Specific commands** — exact `kubectl`, `aws`, `az`, or `gcloud` commands to run for each check
6. **Correlation queries** — Grafana/Datadog queries to correlate infrastructure metrics with the incident timeline

Provide commands appropriate for the {{architecture}} architecture.

nodes.label: Application Investigation
nodes.description: Investigate application-level issues: code bugs, memory leaks, deadlocks, misconfigurations
nodes.template: Service: {{affectedService}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Recent changes: {{recentChanges}}. Monitoring: {{monitoringExists}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are a senior developer investigating an application-level production issue. Generate:
1. **Log analysis** — specific log queries (ELK/Splunk/CloudWatch) to find error patterns, stack traces, and rate changes
2. **APM deep dive** — distributed trace analysis, flame graphs, slow query identification
3. **Memory/CPU profiling** — commands to capture heap dumps, thread dumps, or CPU profiles in production
4. **Dependency health** — database connection pool status, cache hit rates, external API response times
5. **Code correlation** — how to identify the specific code path causing issues (git log, deploy manifest)
6. **Reproduction steps** — safe ways to reproduce in staging or with feature flags

Tailor to the {{architecture}} architecture and {{recentChanges}}.

nodes.label: Dependency Investigation
nodes.description: Investigate external dependency failures: third-party APIs, cloud services, CDN
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an SRE investigating a potential external dependency failure. Generate:
1. **Status page check** — list of status pages to check for common cloud providers and services
2. **Dependency mapping** — identify all upstream and downstream dependencies of {{affectedService}}
3. **Circuit breaker status** — check if circuit breakers have tripped, retry/backoff configuration
4. **Fallback activation** — steps to enable graceful degradation or cached responses
5. **Provider communication** — template for opening a support case with the cloud provider
6. **Mitigation options** — failover to backup region, switch to alternative provider, enable offline mode

Be specific about the dependency architecture.

nodes.label: Escalation Level
nodes.description: Determine if the incident needs further escalation
nodes.condition: Does this need escalation beyond the current responders?

nodes.label: Escalate Incident
nodes.description: Escalate to leadership, additional teams, or vendor support
nodes.template: Incident: {{affectedService}} in {{environment}}. Severity: {{initialSeverity}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Duration so far: ongoing. Team available: {{teamStatus}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an incident commander escalating a production incident. Generate:
1. **Escalation summary** — concise situation report for leadership (what, when, who, impact, current actions)
2. **Teams to page** — which teams need to be involved based on the failure domain and architecture
3. **Customer communication** — status page update template with honest impact description and ETA guidance
4. **Executive briefing** — 3-sentence summary suitable for VP/CTO notification
5. **SLA impact assessment** — calculate time remaining before SLA breach based on severity
6. **Resource requests** — what additional resources or authority is needed

Use standard incident management language (ITIL/SRE).

nodes.label: Apply Remediation
nodes.description: Execute the identified fix and verify recovery
nodes.template: Service: {{affectedService}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Environment: {{environment}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Recent changes: {{recentChanges}}. Monitoring: {{monitoringExists}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an SRE applying a remediation to a production incident. Generate:
1. **Fix implementation** — specific steps to apply the identified fix safely
2. **Change management** — emergency change record template (what, why, risk, rollback plan)
3. **Verification checklist** — ordered checks to confirm the fix resolved the issue:
   - Immediate (0-5 min): error rate, latency, health checks
   - Short-term (5-30 min): throughput recovery, queue drain, cache warm-up
   - Medium-term (30-60 min): SLA metric recovery, customer report resolution
4. **Monitoring adjustments** — temporary alert thresholds or additional dashboards to watch
5. **Communication update** — status page update: 'Monitoring fix' template

Tailor to {{architecture}} architecture.

nodes.label: Post-Mortem Depth
nodes.description: How thorough should the post-incident review be?
nodes.condition: What level of post-mortem is warranted?

nodes.label: Full Post-Mortem
nodes.description: Comprehensive blameless post-mortem with timeline, root cause, and action items
nodes.template: Incident: {{affectedService}} in {{environment}}. Severity: {{initialSeverity}}. Alert source: {{alertSource}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Changes: {{recentChanges}}. Monitoring: {{monitoringExists}}. Runbooks: {{runbooksExist}}. Canary: {{canaryAvailable}}.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are a senior SRE facilitating a blameless post-mortem. Generate a complete post-mortem document:

1. **Incident Summary** — What happened, impact duration, services affected, users impacted
2. **Timeline** — Chronological events from first alert to resolution with timestamps
3. **Root Cause Analysis** — Use the 5 Whys technique to dig to the systemic root cause
4. **Contributing Factors** — What conditions allowed this to happen (gaps in monitoring, testing, process)
5. **What Went Well** — Effective response actions, tools that helped, team coordination
6. **What Went Poorly** — Delayed detection, missing runbooks, communication gaps
7. **Action Items** — Prioritized list with owners and deadlines:
   - P0 (this week): prevent exact recurrence
   - P1 (this sprint): prevent similar incidents
   - P2 (this quarter): systemic improvements
8. **Monitoring Gaps** — New alerts, dashboards, or SLOs to add
9. **Process Improvements** — Changes to on-call, escalation, or communication procedures
10. **Change Freeze Recommendation** — Whether to enforce a deploy freeze and for how long

Be specific to the incident details. Use blameless language throughout.

nodes.label: Quick Retro
nodes.description: Lightweight retrospective for lower-severity incidents
nodes.template: Incident: {{affectedService}}. Severity: {{initialSeverity}}. Symptoms: {{symptoms}}. Changes: {{recentChanges}}. Resolution: completed.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are an SRE writing a quick incident retro for a lower-severity event. Generate:
1. **One-paragraph summary** — what happened and how it was resolved
2. **Root cause** — single sentence identifying the cause
3. **Action items** — 2-3 concrete improvements with owners
4. **Detection improvement** — one thing that would have caught this faster
5. **Runbook update** — what to add to the service runbook

Keep it concise. This is a 15-minute review, not a full post-mortem.

nodes.label: Preventive Measures
nodes.description: Long-term reliability improvements to prevent recurrence
nodes.template: Service: {{affectedService}}. Architecture: {{architecture}}. Root cause area: {{symptoms}}. Monitoring: {{monitoringExists}}. Runbooks: {{runbooksExist}}. Canary: {{canaryAvailable}}. Change freeze preference: high.
nodes.systemPrompt:
You are a reliability engineering lead designing preventive measures after a production incident. Generate:
1. **SLO Definition** — Propose specific SLIs and SLOs for {{affectedService}} (availability, latency, error rate)
2. **Alert Tuning** — New alerts that would have caught this sooner, with specific thresholds and burn-rate windows
3. **Chaos Engineering** — GameDay scenario to test resilience against this failure mode
4. **Architecture Hardening** — Specific changes to make the system more resilient (circuit breakers, bulkheads, retries, fallbacks)
5. **Runbook Updates** — Outline of a new or updated runbook for this failure class
6. **Testing Gaps** — Integration tests, load tests, or failure injection tests to add to CI/CD
7. **Toil Reduction** — Automation opportunities identified during the incident

Be specific and actionable. Include estimated effort for each recommendation.
